sections.title: Water on Earth
sections.content_summary: The chapter opens by establishing that although most of Earth's surface is covered with water, the majority is salty and unfit for drinking or farming. Freshwater — essential for drinking, growing crops, and daily activities — constitutes only a tiny fraction, illustrated by the teaspoon-in-a-glass analogy. All living beings, including people, animals, birds, and plants, depend on freshwater to survive. Traditional water-harvesting structures such as the 'Johads' of Rajasthan are referenced as examples of communities that built small dams to collect rainwater and recharge groundwater.

tasks.task_text: Do you think we can drink the water present in the oceans?
What can ocean water be used for?
Identify the different freshwater sources from the images given below and write their names.

sections.title: Water has Different Forms
sections.content_summary: This section revisits the three states of water — liquid (rain), solid (ice), and vapour (steam) — and builds understanding through hands-on observation. Students observe water droplets forming on a cold steel glass containing ice cubes, noting condensation, melting, and evaporation. A structured observation table asks students to record changes when ice melts, water boils, and water is left in sunlight for three days, reinforcing that water can transform between states and return to its liquid form.

tasks.task_text: Take a steel glass. Put some ice cubes in it. Observe the small water droplets forming on the glass. (a) Where do these water droplets come from? (b) What happens to the ice cubes after they are left in the glass for some time? (c) If we heat water, what will happen to it? In the above activity, what forms of water do you see? [Observing Changes table: I Observe — Ice melting / Water boiling / Water in sunlight for three days]

sections.title: Water Cycle
sections.content_summary: The section explains the continuous circular movement of water in nature through the water cycle. The Sun's heat causes water from oceans, rivers, and other sources to evaporate into water vapour; this vapour cools to form clouds, which release rain, snow, and hail that flow back into rivers, lakes, and oceans. A sealed transparent bag activity models the water cycle at small scale: coloured water placed in sunlight evaporates, condenses inside the bag, and drips back down, mirroring the natural cycle. The Fishing Cat of India's wetlands is cited as an example of wildlife dependent on freshwater ecosystems.

tasks.task_text: Take a transparent bag. Use a marker to draw the sun, clouds, trees and arrows on it as shown in the picture. Fill one-third of it with coloured water. Seal the bag tightly to prevent any leaks. Place it in the sunlight. After a few hours, observe the changes inside the bag. [I Observe / I Wonder table]

sections.title: Groundwater
sections.content_summary: This section explains how rainwater that soaks into the soil percolates through layers of soil and rock to form groundwater stored deep underground. Wells, borewells, tube wells, and handpumps are used to draw this water for human use. An activity with a glass of soil models the percolation process. A key point is that paved and cemented urban surfaces prevent rainwater infiltration, stopping groundwater recharge; open areas, soak pits, ponds, and trees help rainwater return underground. The Statue of Unity in India is mentioned as a factual aside.

tasks.task_text: Take a transparent glass. Fill half of it with soil. Slowly pour water into the soil using a spoon. Observe what happens.
Tick the image that will help in groundwater recharge. [Concrete road / Green lawn / Paved area]

sections.title: Surface Water
sections.content_summary: Rivers, ponds, and lakes are natural sources of freshwater in addition to groundwater. The section explains that rivers typically begin in mountains and flow downhill, with their path and speed shaped by the landforms they encounter; water flow can also change the shape of land over time. A mustard-seed-on-crumpled-newspaper activity models how rivers follow the contours of the land and spread out. A map of India shows major rivers and their directions of flow — some towards the Bay of Bengal, others towards the Arabian Sea. The Luni River of Rajasthan, which ends in the Rann of Kutch and does not drain into a sea, is highlighted as a unique example. Hiware Bazar in Maharashtra is noted as a successful water-positive village through community-led watershed management and rainwater harvesting.

tasks.task_text: Take a sheet from a newspaper and crumple it to create folds. Take another sheet and place it over the crumpled paper. Press it down so that the slopes are gentle. Now, slowly pour the mustard seeds from the cup on to the highest point of the slope. Observe the movement of the seeds. [Questions: 1. What did you observe about the mustard seeds? 2. Are they moving in a straight line, or do they spread out in different directions? 3. Are they collecting in some areas?]
On the basis of Activity 6 with mustard seeds, discuss how some rivers flow towards the Arabian Sea while some flow towards the Bay of Bengal.

Follow the Flow! In the map, you can see the rivers flowing in different directions. Some flow into the Bay of Bengal and some into the Arabian Sea. Observe and fill in the table: Name of the River / Moves towards the Bay of Bengal / Moves towards the Arabian Sea.

sections.title: Life in Water
sections.content_summary: This section explores the rich variety of plants and animals living in freshwater habitats. Students compare characteristics of land animals and aquatic animals (e.g., fins, gills) and learn about specific freshwater organisms — dragonfly, water scorpion, pond heron, freshwater turtle, water snake, reeds, lotus, water lily, and water hyacinth — through an information card. The waxy coating on leaves is explored through an activity that models how waterproofing protects aquatic plants. A food chain game (Activity 10) introduces interdependence among freshwater organisms. The section emphasises that aquatic plants and animals have special adaptations and that preserving freshwater ecosystems is essential for life.

tasks.task_text: Complete the following table discussing with peers about the characteristics of animals on land and animals in water. [Cannot breathe in water / Have fins to swim — and other characteristics]

Visit a local water body like a pond, lake or an aquarium with your teacher or parents, and observe life in and around the water body. 1. Based on your observations, complete the following table [Name of the Bird or Animal / Mouth or Beaks / Movement through Legs, Feet, Fins / Rough Diagram]. 2. Draw the plants that you saw during the visit and label them with their local name [Types / Names / Local Name].
Take a piece of paper and put a drop of water on it. Observe. Colour the paper with a wax crayon. Now, put a drop of water on it. Do you observe any change? Write your observation.

Who Eats Whom? — A River Food Chain Game. Distribute slips of paper to students. Each student writes what they choose to be (for example, small fish, big fish, frog, bird, human, crocodile, otter, etc.). Ask the students to think about what they eat, and who eats them. Use a string to connect the students who depend on each other for food. Discuss what would happen if one animal disappears.

sections.title: Let us reflect
sections.content_summary: The closing reflection section consolidates learning from the entire chapter through a mix of matching, reasoning, and creative tasks. Students match water forms (ocean, snow, steam, rainwater) to their properties; reason about why most Earth water is unusable; explain why living beings cluster near water bodies; predict consequences of a two-year drought; compare rainwater fate in forests versus cities; and design a water-conserving home or school. A paper-folding origami fish activity concludes the chapter, connecting craft with the aquatic life theme.

tasks.task_text: Match the following: (i) Ocean water → (a) Solid form of water / (b) Vapour form of water / (c) Not fit for drinking / (d) Freshwater; (ii) Snow; (iii) Steam; (iv) Rainwater
Why do you think most of the water on Earth cannot be used for drinking or farming?
Large number of living beings live near water bodies. Why?
What would happen if it did not rain in your region for two years?
What do you think happens to rainwater in a forest compared to a city?
Can you design a house or school that conserves water wisely? What would it include?
Let us make a fish by folding a piece of paper. [Origami fish — Steps 1 to 6]

dual_strand.natural: Water forms, water cycle, groundwater percolation, surface water flow, and freshwater habitats with aquatic biodiversity
dual_strand.human_cultural: Water conservation traditions (Johads of Rajasthan), community watershed management (Hiware Bazar), and civic responsibility for freshwater stewardship
